metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure ERCP consult for choledocholithiasis in altered (Roux-en-Y) anatomy

planted_discrepancies.note: advanced routing mandatory; the requested standard approach must be changed before scheduling

planted_discrepancies.note: limb lengths determine feasibility of enteroscopy-assisted vs lap-assisted approach; op note must be obtained

patient confirms daily bariatric multivitamin and monthly B12

prior_notes.text: New patient establishing GI care. History of Roux-en-Y gastric bypass in 2019 (performed at outside facility; operative report not available — records requested). Post-bypass course uncomplicated; weight down approximately 110 lb from preoperative baseline and stable. On bariatric multivitamin and monthly intramuscular B12 for documented B12 deficiency. No abdominal pain at this visit. Plan: continue supplementation, obtain outside operative records to document anatomy and limb lengths.

MRCP: 9 mm calculus in the distal common bile duct with upstream CBD dilation to 11 mm. Gallbladder contains several small stones without wall thickening. No intrahepatic ductal dilation. Pancreatic duct normal. Impression: choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction. Note: surgically altered upper GI anatomy (Roux-en-Y) — recommend GI/advanced endoscopy consultation regarding access approach.

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: 9 mm distal CBD calculus with CBD dilation to 11 mm; cholelithiasis; choledocholithiasis with obstruction. Surgically altered (Roux-en-Y) upper GI anatomy — advise consultation regarding biliary access approach.

transcript:
MA: Hi Mr. Williams, let me room you and grab your vitals before the doctor comes in. Height's five foot ten, weight's one eighty-one pounds. Temp 36.7, heart rate 72, breathing 14, blood pressure 122 over 78, and your oxygen's 98 percent on room air.
DR: Good morning, Mr. Williams — I'm the advanced endoscopist. You were referred for a procedure to clear a stone in your bile duct. Tell me what's been going on.
PT: Yeah, I had this pain up here on the right side, comes in waves, and my urine went dark for a couple days. The scan showed a stone stuck in the duct.
DR: That fits with your labs — your bilirubin and liver numbers are up, and the MRCP shows a stone with the duct backed up. Now, the important part for me: you had gastric bypass surgery, is that right?
PT: Right, 2019. Best thing I ever did — I lost about a hundred and ten pounds and I've kept it off.
DR: That's great, and it's stayed stable. The reason I'm asking is that the bypass rearranges your anatomy. A regular ERCP goes down through the mouth to the duct, but after a Roux-en-Y that route doesn't reach anymore. So the standard scope they wrote on the referral won't work for you — we'd need either a scope-assisted approach that goes the long way around, or working together with a surgeon who makes a small opening in the old stomach so I can reach the duct that way.
PT: Oh. Nobody told me it'd be different.
DR: It changes the plan quite a bit. To pick the safest route I really need the operative report from your bypass — specifically how long the limbs of the bypass were. Do you know where that surgery was done?
PT: It was at a hospital across the bay, but I don't have any of the paperwork. I honestly wouldn't know the lengths, they never went into that with me.
DR: No problem — we'll request those records before we schedule. One more thing: any blood thinners?
PT: I take a blood thinner every day, yeah.
DR: Okay, which one — something like warfarin, or one of the newer ones?
PT: No, no, it's the fish oil. The omega-3 capsules. My last doctor called it a natural blood thinner so that's what I say.
DR: Ah — good to clarify. Fish oil isn't a true blood thinner, so there's nothing there we need to stop for the procedure. Are you on anything else?
PT: Just my bariatric multivitamin every day and the B12 shot once a month.
DR: Perfect. So the plan is: get your operative records, confirm the approach, and then schedule you. My coordinator will call you.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Williams is a 41-year-old man referred for ERCP for choledocholithiasis. He reports intermittent right-upper-quadrant colicky pain and a few days of dark urine. He has a history of Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (2019, outside facility) with sustained ~110 lb weight loss; obesity resolved and weight stable. He describes taking a daily "blood thinner," which on direct questioning is over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3), not a therapeutic anticoagulant or antiplatelet. He confirms daily bariatric multivitamin and monthly intramuscular B12. He does not know his bypass limb lengths and has no operative paperwork.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 82 kg (181 lb), BMI 25.9. Labs (2026-07-15): total bilirubin 3.1 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 268 U/L, ALT 142 U/L, INR 1.0 — obstructive pattern. MRCP (2026-07-06): 9 mm distal CBD calculus with CBD dilation to 11 mm; cholelithiasis; surgically altered Roux-en-Y anatomy.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction in a patient with Roux-en-Y gastric bypass — CONFIRMED indication for biliary drainage.
- ALTERED ANATOMY: standard per-oral ERCP is not feasible. Approach must be advanced — either device/enteroscopy-assisted ERCP or laparoscopic-assisted transgastric ERCP. Referral request for standard ERCP amended; case routed to advanced endoscopy.
- Operative report from 2019 RYGB (outside facility) NOT in chart — obtain to document limb lengths, which determine feasibility of enteroscopy-assisted vs laparoscopic-assisted approach. Records request initiated; multidisciplinary discussion with bariatric/general surgery.
- Medication reconciliation: daily "blood thinner" clarified as fish oil (omega-3), NOT a therapeutic anticoagulant — no periprocedural anticoagulation hold required. Continue bariatric multivitamin and monthly B12.
- Do not schedule until operative records reviewed and approach confirmed; coordinator to contact patient. Anesthesia clearance and periprocedural instructions to follow once approach selected.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• A stone blocking your bile duct, causing right-sided pain and dark urine
• How your 2019 gastric bypass changes the way we reach the bile duct
• The records and steps we need before scheduling your procedure

Next steps
• Because of your bypass, a standard ERCP through the mouth will not reach your bile duct. We will use a special approach (a longer specialized scope, or working with a surgeon through the old stomach).
• We are requesting the operative report from your 2019 bypass surgery. We need it to choose the safest approach before we schedule.
• Your daily fish oil is not a true blood thinner, so you do not need to stop it for the procedure.
• Keep taking your bariatric multivitamin daily and your monthly B12 shot.
• Our coordinator will call you once your records are reviewed. Call sooner for fever, worsening pain, or yellowing of the eyes or skin.
